CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper is generally well organized into introduction, methods, experiments and conclusion and states the main contribution early (e.g. “RFNs overcome the shortcomings of FABIA” in the Introduction). Notation and equations are largely presented (Eqs. (1)-(4)) and understandable, but some symbols/constraints use odd characters (e.g. “8i : µi 0”) and a few definitions/references are unclear or incomplete. Figures/tables are referenced (Fig.1, Table 1/2, Fig.2) but are not shown in the provided text and the description of evaluation (e.g. “Step (3) penalizes...”) omits detail, reducing clarity. Writing is mostly readable, but the manuscript contains duplicated paragraphs, LaTeX/artifact remnants and minor grammatical/formatting issues that distract from the presentation. | B(3): The paper has a logical organization (Introduction → Methods → Experiments → Conclusion) and clearly states the main contribution early (motivating FABIA shortcomings and proposing RFNs to address them). However, the notation and formulae are inconsistently presented and sometimes garbled (e.g. Eq. (3) 'I + W T 1W 1 W T 1 vi' and other malformed expressions), which hampers readability. Figures and tables are referenced (Fig.1, Table 1/2, Fig.2) but are not present in the provided text, and the manuscript contains duplicated/mangled paragraphs and formatting errors in the later sections, indicating the need for careful copy‑editing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized into introduction, methods, experiments and conclusion and states the main contribution early (e.g. “RFNs overcome the shortcomings of FABIA” in the Introduction). Notation and equations are largely presented (Eqs. (1)-(4)) and understandable, but some symbols/constraints use odd characters (e.g. “8i : µi 0”) and a few definitions/references are unclear or incomplete. Figures/tables are referenced (Fig.1, Table 1/2, Fig.2) but are not shown in the provided text and the description of evaluation (e.g. “Step (3) penalizes...”) omits detail, reducing clarity. Writing is mostly readable, but the manuscript contains duplicated paragraphs, LaTeX/artifact remnants and minor grammatical/formatting issues that distract from the presentation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper has a logical organization (Introduction → Methods → Experiments → Conclusion) and clearly states the main contribution early (motivating FABIA shortcomings and proposing RFNs to address them). However, the notation and formulae are inconsistently presented and sometimes garbled (e.g. Eq. (3) 'I + W T 1W 1 W T 1 vi' and other malformed expressions), which hampers readability. Figures and tables are referenced (Fig.1, Table 1/2, Fig.2) but are not present in the provided text, and the manuscript contains duplicated/mangled paragraphs and formatting errors in the later sections, indicating the need for careful copy‑editing.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The core RFN machinery is presented as prior work (‘‘recently introduced Rectified Factor Networks (RFNs; (Clevert et al., 2015))’’) and this paper mainly applies/adapts RFNs to biclustering rather than introducing a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. The paper’s novel elements are application-specific extensions (Laplace prior on weights, Eq. (5)–(6), and using dropout to decorrelate/encourage sparsity) and empirical validation across many benchmarks (Sections 2.2–3), which are useful but constitute a non-trivial but incremental advance over existing methods (e.g., FABIA). The claim of transferring rectification to factor-analysis posteriors is credited to the RFN prior work, so the contribution here is best seen as a notable application and modest technical extension rather than a highly original methodological breakthrough. | B(3): The paper applies an existing model (Rectified Factor Networks, Clevert et al., 2015) to biclustering and introduces practical extensions (Laplace prior on weights, dropout to decorrelate units) rather than a wholly new generative framework — i.e. an application-driven, non-trivial but incremental advance. Biclustering itself is a well-studied problem and the main novelty is methodological adaptation (sections 2.1–2.2) and scalability claims (many code units, rectified posterior means yielding exact zeros). The authors clearly contrast RFN-based biclustering with FABIA and other prior work and quantify improvements experimentally, but core ideas (rectified posteriors / RFNs) are not introduced here and much novelty is in engineering/regularization choices rather than a fundamentally new formulation.

reviewer_a.justification: The core RFN machinery is presented as prior work (‘‘recently introduced Rectified Factor Networks (RFNs; (Clevert et al., 2015))’’) and this paper mainly applies/adapts RFNs to biclustering rather than introducing a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. The paper’s novel elements are application-specific extensions (Laplace prior on weights, Eq. (5)–(6), and using dropout to decorrelate/encourage sparsity) and empirical validation across many benchmarks (Sections 2.2–3), which are useful but constitute a non-trivial but incremental advance over existing methods (e.g., FABIA). The claim of transferring rectification to factor-analysis posteriors is credited to the RFN prior work, so the contribution here is best seen as a notable application and modest technical extension rather than a highly original methodological breakthrough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies an existing model (Rectified Factor Networks, Clevert et al., 2015) to biclustering and introduces practical extensions (Laplace prior on weights, dropout to decorrelate units) rather than a wholly new generative framework — i.e. an application-driven, non-trivial but incremental advance. Biclustering itself is a well-studied problem and the main novelty is methodological adaptation (sections 2.1–2.2) and scalability claims (many code units, rectified posterior means yielding exact zeros). The authors clearly contrast RFN-based biclustering with FABIA and other prior work and quantify improvements experimentally, but core ideas (rectified posteriors / RFNs) are not introduced here and much novelty is in engineering/regularization choices rather than a fundamentally new formulation.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper presents a plausible model and extensive empirical comparisons (Sec. 3.1–3.3) against many baselines, which supports the main empirical claims, but key methodological details and evidence are missing (no p‑values / effect sizes shown, tuning on auxiliary toys risks bias). Several mathematical expressions appear incorrect or mistyped (Eq. (3) gives a posterior mean/covariance in a form inconsistent with standard factor analysis, and Eq. (4) / its solution via a single projected‑Newton step is asserted to “guarantee the correct solution” without proof). Claims about scalability to “thousands of code units” are not demonstrated in the experiments (e.g. RFN used 13 units in benchmarks), and the weight‑update with Laplace prior is stated without derivation. These issues raise moderate technical concerns that do not fully invalidate the contribution but require clarification and additional evidence. | B(3): The paper states clear empirical claims and reports comparisons to many baselines (Sec. 3.1–3.3) and significance testing, but the provided text omits the actual numerical results/tables and details of the statistical analysis. The mathematical presentation contains apparent errors/typos (the Gaussian posterior mean/covariance formulas as printed are not the standard (I+W^T Ψ^{-1}W)^{-1}W^TΨ^{-1}v form and a + appears where an inverse is expected) and the key claim that a single projected-Newton step "guarantees the correct solution" is stated without proof here. The experimental methodology is broadly reasonable (many baselines, synthetic and real data) but has gaps and inconsistencies (e.g. earlier claim RFNs scale to thousands of units while experiments used 13 units, limited details on parameter tuning, multiple-testing control, and exact consensus-score computation are missing). Given these mathematical and methodological gaps but otherwise plausible empirical evaluation, I rate the paper 3 (some concerns about completeness/correctness of derivations and missing experimental detail).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a plausible model and extensive empirical comparisons (Sec. 3.1–3.3) against many baselines, which supports the main empirical claims, but key methodological details and evidence are missing (no p‑values / effect sizes shown, tuning on auxiliary toys risks bias). Several mathematical expressions appear incorrect or mistyped (Eq. (3) gives a posterior mean/covariance in a form inconsistent with standard factor analysis, and Eq. (4) / its solution via a single projected‑Newton step is asserted to “guarantee the correct solution” without proof). Claims about scalability to “thousands of code units” are not demonstrated in the experiments (e.g. RFN used 13 units in benchmarks), and the weight‑update with Laplace prior is stated without derivation. These issues raise moderate technical concerns that do not fully invalidate the contribution but require clarification and additional evidence.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear empirical claims and reports comparisons to many baselines (Sec. 3.1–3.3) and significance testing, but the provided text omits the actual numerical results/tables and details of the statistical analysis. The mathematical presentation contains apparent errors/typos (the Gaussian posterior mean/covariance formulas as printed are not the standard (I+W^T Ψ^{-1}W)^{-1}W^TΨ^{-1}v form and a + appears where an inverse is expected) and the key claim that a single projected-Newton step "guarantees the correct solution" is stated without proof here. The experimental methodology is broadly reasonable (many baselines, synthetic and real data) but has gaps and inconsistencies (e.g. earlier claim RFNs scale to thousands of units while experiments used 13 units, limited details on parameter tuning, multiple-testing control, and exact consensus-score computation are missing). Given these mathematical and methodological gaps but otherwise plausible empirical evaluation, I rate the paper 3 (some concerns about completeness/correctness of derivations and missing experimental detail).
